paragraph: A research team at the Haldervane Centre for Electrochemical Organic Synthesis, embedded in Thornton University of Chemistry in Thornton, United Kingdom, has demonstrated the electrochemical Birch reduction of aromatic compounds to cyclohexadienes using a proton-coupled electron transfer mechanism in tetrahydrofuran-tert-butanol solvent mixtures at minus 20 degrees Celsius, achieving yields of 88-96 percent and diastereomeric ratios up to 14:1 for six pharmaceutically relevant substrates at a cathode current density of 25 milliamperes per square centimeter. The report, authored by Dr. Priya Krishnamurthy-Singh and published in Electroorganic Synthesis on July 8, 2025, revealed that replacing lithium with a magnesium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide electrolyte at 0.3 molar concentration increased the proton source selectivity and reduced proton transfer to the secondary radical anion intermediate, improving cyclohexadiene yield by 18 percentage points compared to the lithium electrolyte control. The undivided electrochemical cell operated continuously for 50 hours using a reticulated vitreous carbon cathode and a platinum anode, producing 127 grams of 1,4-cyclohexadiene carboxylate pharmaceutical intermediate with 94.3 percent yield and 99.1 percent purity, eliminating the dissolved metal waste stream generated by the stoichiometric chemical Birch reduction process used previously at the same facility.

paragraph: Chemists at the Telbright Industrial Green Chemistry Center, a division of Norwick University of Applied Sciences in Norwick, Finland, have reported a solvent-free mechanochemical procedure using copper oxide as catalyst that performs Chan-Lam coupling reactions between boronic acids and amines in a planetary ball mill at 300 revolutions per minute for 30 minutes at ambient temperature, giving biaryl amine products in yields of 82-97 percent and with less than 0.4 parts per million residual copper contamination in the product after simple washings. The work, supervised by Dr. Mikael Ahlqvist-Bergman and appearing in Mechanochemical Synthesis and Catalysis on March 4, 2025, showed that ball-milling created fresh copper oxide surfaces at a rate of 0.12 square meters per gram per minute, maintaining catalytic activity equivalent to 0.8 mole percent dissolved CuO catalyst in solution-phase conditions as quantified by electrochemical copper dissolution assays. The substrate scope encompassed 34 boronic acid-amine pairs including electron-rich and electron-poor examples, and the reaction mass efficiency of 84 percent far exceeded the 23 percent calculated for the conventional solution-phase analogue due to elimination of solvent waste. A kilogram-scale mechanochemical campaign produced 0.94 kilograms of a key drug synthesis intermediate in 93 percent yield with a process mass intensity of 4.1, compared to 38.7 for the established homogeneous copper catalysis route.

paragraph: Scientists at the Valemonde Biorefinery Catalysis Unit, part of Lorraine Coastal University of Science and Technology in Lorraine, France, have synthesized a phosphorus-doped carbon catalyst derived from pyrolysis of chitosan at 900 degrees Celsius under nitrogen atmosphere that converts levulinic acid, a platform chemical obtained from cellulose hydrolysis, to gamma-valerolactone at 150 degrees Celsius and 30 bar hydrogen pressure with 98.7 percent conversion and 96.4 percent gamma-valerolactone selectivity at a liquid hourly space velocity of 2.8 per hour. The study, directed by Professor Marie-Claire Bonneau-Duchamp and published in Biomass Conversion and Biorefinery Chemistry on October 6, 2024, showed that phosphorus at 2.9 weight percent in the carbon matrix created electron-withdrawing surface groups that increased the catalyst turnover frequency for the hydrogenation of the keto group in levulinic acid by 3.7-fold relative to undoped carbon, while suppressing the formation of 2-methyltetrahydrofuran by-product to below 1.8 percent. The catalyst was prepared from waste crustacean shells sourced from the Lorraine fishing industry, embodying a circular bioeconomy approach, and scaled at 25 kilograms per batch in a rotary kiln at Valemonde Carbon Materials. The team operated a fixed-bed pilot reactor for 800 hours at Lorraine BioFuels Facility processing 4.2 tonnes of levulinic acid total.

paragraph: A team at the Halverstrom Molecular Simulation and Experimental Catalysis Group, affiliated with Esbjerg Technical University in Esbjerg, Denmark, has combined machine-learning interatomic potential simulations with experimental validation to discover a nickel-gallium intermetallic compound with composition Ni5Ga3 that catalyzes the selective hydrogenation of acetylene to ethylene in the presence of excess ethylene at 60 degrees Celsius and 2 bar pressure with 99.6 percent acetylene conversion and ethylene selectivity of 97.4 percent, outperforming the commercial silver-palladium catalyst benchmarked at the same conditions. The research, headed by Dr. Anders Kromann-Vestergaard and published in Computational and Experimental Catalysis on June 27, 2025, used a neural network potential trained on 42,000 density functional theory-derived configurations of the Ni-Ga surface to predict the ensemble of surface terminations stable under reaction conditions, identifying the Ni5Ga3(010) facet as uniquely selective due to isolated Ni sites surrounded by inactive Ga that prevent di-sigma acetylene adsorption and the sequential hydrogenation to ethane. Physical vapor deposition was used to prepare model Ni5Ga3 surfaces and nanoparticle analogues on alumina, confirming the computational predictions with a selectivity variation of less than 2 percentage points across six independently prepared samples. The nanoparticle catalyst with average diameter 6.8 nanometers operated for 600 hours on stream without deactivation.

Dr. Anders Kromann-Vestergaard's team trained a neural network potential on 42,000 DFT surface configurations for the Ni-Ga system, used it to predict stable surface terminations under reaction conditions, and identified the Ni5Ga3(010) facet as uniquely selective because isolated Ni sites surrounded by inactive Ga prevent di-sigma acetylene adsorption. They then prepared model surfaces and alumina-supported nanoparticles by physical vapor deposition and confirmed the prediction: 99.6 percent acetylene conversion and 97.4 percent ethylene selectivity at 60 degrees Celsius and 2 bar, outperforming commercial silver-palladium. Six independently prepared samples gave consistent selectivity within 2 percentage points, and the 6.8-nanometer nanoparticle catalyst ran 600 hours without deactivation. Published in Computational and Experimental Catalysis on June 27, 2025, this paper shows that ML-guided intermetallic discovery is becoming a reliable approach to solving industrially relevant selectivity problems.

The 99.6 percent conversion and 97.4 percent ethylene selectivity, versus commercial Ag-Pd benchmarks at the same conditions, arise from the Ni5Ga3(010) facet structure where isolated Ni sites surrounded by inactive Ga prevent di-sigma acetylene adsorption and sequential hydrogenation to ethane. The 600-hour nanoparticle stability on stream eliminates the deactivation concern. paragraph: Researchers at the Feldgate Electrochemical Engineering Laboratory, part of Portlandia University of Chemical Engineering in Portlandia, Sweden, have constructed a proton exchange membrane electrolyzer cell incorporating an iridium-ruthenium oxide anode that achieves 2 amperes per square centimeter water oxidation current at a cell voltage of 1.71 volts at 80 degrees Celsius, corresponding to an energy efficiency of 87.3 percent on a lower heating value basis of hydrogen. The work, spearheaded by Dr. Kristina Lund-Bjornsson and disseminated in Electrochemical Hydrogen Production on February 19, 2025, demonstrated that an iridium-to-ruthenium atomic ratio of 2:1 optimized the balance between oxygen evolution catalytic activity and corrosion resistance under harsh anodic conditions, with the mixed oxide maintaining 93.4 percent of initial activity at 2 amperes per square centimeter over 3,000 hours of continuous operation at 80 degrees Celsius compared to 71.2 percent retention for pure ruthenium oxide under identical conditions. The catalyst was deposited by reactive co-sputtering onto a titanium fiber paper current collector at a total metal loading of 1.2 milligrams per square centimeter and characterized by synchrotron X-ray diffraction confirming a homogeneous rutile-phase solid solution structure without phase separation. A 10-cell stack of 400-square-centimeter cells produced 1.8 kilograms of hydrogen per hour at the Portlandia Green Hydrogen Research Facility during a 120-day operational trial.

paragraph: Chemists at the Grenmore Laboratory for Atom-Economical Synthesis, embedded within Ashvale College of Chemical Technology in Ashvale, India, have developed a rhodium-catalyzed C-H borylation protocol using bis(pinacolato)diboron that functionalizes unactivated arenes at the meta position with 94.7 percent regioselectivity and 89.4 percent isolated yield using 2 mole percent Rh catalyst at 120 degrees Celsius and 1 bar nitrogen atmosphere, eliminating the need for directing groups and pre-functionalized substrates required by conventional ortho-selective methods. The findings, authored by Professor Pradeep Chakraborty-Sharma and published in Atom-Economical Organic Synthesis on August 23, 2024, revealed that a bulky phosphine ligand with a cone angle of 163 degrees directed boron delivery to the meta position by steric shielding of ortho and para sites, as confirmed by noncovalent interaction plot analysis of density functional theory-optimized transition state geometries. The protocol demonstrated complete tolerance to ester, ketone, fluorine, and trifluoromethyl functional groups across 41 substrates and was extended to gram-scale preparation of five aryl boronate ester building blocks with yields ranging from 86.4 to 92.8 percent. An E-factor analysis showed a value of 7.3 for the new meta-selective process compared to 34.8 for a multi-step sequence via diazonium salt intermediates that achieves the same product, quantifying a substantial reduction in waste generation per gram of product.

paragraph: A research group at the Caldenvast Industrial Biotransformation Centre, housed within Marindal University of Fermentation Science in Marindal, Brazil, has developed a continuous enzymatic membrane reactor integrating an immobilized Candida analogue lipase on polypropylene hollow fibers that produces flavor ester geranyl acetate from geraniol and acetic anhydride at a productivity of 2.4 kilograms per liter of reactor volume per day with 97.8 percent conversion and ester purity of 99.1 percent after inline distillation. The work, led by Dr. Fernanda Alves-Nobrega and appearing in Biocatalytic Flavor and Fragrance Chemistry on May 20, 2025, demonstrated that the hollow-fiber configuration with a membrane surface area of 8.4 square meters per liter of reactor volume enhanced mass transfer coefficients 4.8-fold compared to stirred-tank immobilized enzyme reactors, enabling operation at substrate concentrations of 1.5 molar without substrate inhibition that occurs above 0.8 molar in stirred-tank configurations. Enzyme half-life in the hollow-fiber system was 74 days at 40 degrees Celsius compared to 12 days for the free enzyme at 35 degrees Celsius, attributed to the stabilizing effect of covalent attachment via glutaraldehyde crosslinking at 0.25 percent concentration. The pilot system at Caldenvast Aroma Chemicals produced 840 kilograms of geranyl acetate over a 90-day campaign for food and cosmetic applications, with water activity maintained at 0.18 using molecular sieve desiccant packets to suppress ester hydrolysis.

paragraph: Investigators at the Nordvelt Centre for Catalytic Nitrogen Oxide Reduction, part of Solviken University of Environmental Chemistry in Solviken, Finland, have developed a copper-exchanged chabazite zeolite catalyst designated Cu-CHA-NV with a copper content of 3.1 weight percent that achieves 99.2 percent NOx conversion in selective catalytic reduction with ammonia at 230 degrees Celsius and space velocity of 80,000 per hour in diesel engine exhaust containing 10 percent water and 25 parts per million sulfur dioxide, demonstrating superior low-temperature activity compared to commercial Cu-SSZ-13 benchmarked at 91.4 percent conversion under identical conditions. The study, supervised by Professor Aino Makinen-Saarinen and published in Automotive Emissions Catalysis on January 21, 2025, revealed that a dual-copper speciation consisting of 62 percent isolated Cu2+ ions at six-membered ring sites and 38 percent Cu-OH species at eight-membered ring sites, quantified by continuous wave electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy at 10 kelvin, was the key structural feature responsible for the enhanced activity at temperatures below 250 degrees Celsius. Hydrothermal aging at 800 degrees Celsius for 16 hours in 10 percent steam caused only 7.4 percent reduction in NOx conversion at 200 degrees Celsius, compared to 19.3 percent for the commercial reference, attributed to the narrower chabazite cage topology resisting copper migration and agglomeration. The catalyst was manufactured at 500 kilogram scale using a continuous ion-exchange process at Nordvelt Catalyst Manufacturing and evaluated in engine bench tests over 1,000 hours representing 100,000 kilometers of equivalent vehicle service.
